headline: Ancient Marsupial Branch Rewrites Australia's Evolutionary Story
meta_description: Scientists discover a previously unknown marsupial order that survived for 35 million years, challenging our understanding of how Australia's unique mammals ev…
summary_short: Fossils from Queensland reveal a lost branch of marsupials that survived for 35 million years alongside modern species, rewriting Australia's evolutionary history.
body:
Teeth and jaw fragments from Queensland caves have just revealed one of evolution's best-kept secrets: an entirely unknown order of marsupials that thrived in Australia for approximately 35 million years.

The three newly identified species, discovered at the Riversleigh World Heritage Area, belonged to an order researchers are calling Keeunamorphia. These small insect eaters, weighing between 25 and 200 grams, lived in dense rainforests around 18 million years ago—a landscape almost unrecognizable from Queensland's dry terrain today.

What makes this discovery genuinely surprising is what it reveals about marsupial evolution. Scientists have long believed that Australia's 160 modern marsupial species descended from a single ancestral lineage. The fossil evidence, however, tells a messier story. These primitive creatures appear to have split off early from the main marsupial family tree and then survived virtually unchanged for millions of years, quietly coexisting with other evolving species.

"Whatever these things were, they seemed to be doing their own thing and surviving well enough alongside them," explains Dr. Tim Churchill of UNSW.

This discovery doesn't just add another branch to the marsupial family tree—it suggests that Australia's unique mammals have a far more complicated origin story than scientists previously imagined. The gaps in the fossil record are finally beginning to fill in.
